---
title: "A Curated Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024: Six Sources, Multilingual Retrieval, and Grey Literature"
author:
  - name: "Minh Ha-Duong\\thanks{Directeur de recherche, CIRED, CNRS. Correspondence: minh.ha-duong@cnrs.fr}"
    orcid: "0000-0001-9988-2100"
    affiliation:
      - name: "CIRED"
      - name: "CNRS"
date: "2026-03-26"
metadata-files: [data-paper-vars.yml]
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
abstract: |
  This data paper presents a curated, multilingual corpus of
  {{< meta corpus_total >}} works on climate finance published between 1990
  and 2024. The dataset is assembled from
  {{< meta corpus_sources >}} complementary sources that combine academic databases,
  institutional repositories, and grey literature, enabling broader coverage
  than single-source bibliometric studies. A multilingual retrieval strategy
  based on an eight-language keyword taxonomy is used to capture relevant
  works across linguistic contexts, while a reproducible pipeline integrates
  deduplication, metadata harmonisation, and quality filtering. The resulting
  corpus includes a citation network derived from Crossref and OpenAlex, as
  well as pre-computed multilingual embeddings to support cross-lingual
  analysis.
keywords: |
  climate finance -- bibliometric corpus -- multilingual --
  sentence-transformer embeddings -- scientometrics -- history of economic thought
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
---

<!-- Target journal: Research Data Journal for the Humanities and Social Sciences (RDJ4HSS) -->
<!-- Format: Data Paper, max 2,500 words, diamond OA, IISG/Openjournals -->
<!-- Structure follows Petram & Kruizinga (2024) exemplar: Introduction, Method, Data, Concluding Remarks -->

- Related dataset: "A Curated Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990--2024: Six Sources, Multilingual Retrieval, and Grey Literature" with DOI [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130) in repository "Zenodo"

## 1. Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Climate finance---the financial flows directed at mitigating and adapting to climate change---has become one of the most politically salient objects in international economic governance, yet the scholarship that analyses it remains bibliographically fragmented [@roberts_weikmans2017; @michaelowa2007]. Academic publications are dispersed across databases with different coverage profiles. Institutional reports from the OECD, UNFCCC, World Bank, and Climate Policy Initiative are absent from standard academic databases. The literature is also multilingual: while English dominates, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German traditions contribute perspectives that monolingual corpora miss entirely.

Pioneering bibliometric work has begun to map this terrain. @care_weber2023 survey sustainable finance using Scopus; @shang_jin2023 analyse climate finance from Web of Science; @maria_etal2023 map green finance with network analysis. These studies rely on a single database each, cover English only, and do not publish reusable datasets.

Our dataset addresses these limitations through {{< meta corpus_sources >}} complementary sources, a documented quality-filtering pipeline, and sentence-transformer embeddings that place all works in a shared semantic space regardless of language. Because "climate finance" is a contested category whose boundaries are themselves an object of study [@weikmans_roberts2019], we err on the side of inclusion and publish the full corpus with quality flags, letting users draw their own boundary. The dataset was constructed for a history-of-economic-thought study of how climate finance crystallised as a category of economic governance [Ha-Duong, under review], but its scope makes it reusable for topic modelling, citation network analysis, bibliometric mapping, and cross-lingual studies.

## 2. Method {#sec-method}

### 2.1 Sources {#sec-sources}

The corpus assembles academic and grey literature from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} sources with complementary coverage profiles (@tbl-sources). Four are automated or semi-automated (reproducible from the scripts and an internet connection); two require manual export from restricted platforms.

| Source | Automation | Coverage |
|--------|------------|----------|
| OpenAlex [@priem_etal2022] | Automated (free API) | Primary academic source (indexes Crossref, DOAJ, PubMed, and more): tiered keyword search |
| ISTEX | Automated (public API) | CNRS national text-mining platform (multi-publisher) |
| Grey literature | Hybrid (curated seed + World Bank API) | 16 curated reports [e.g., @buchner2013] + World Bank repository |
| Teaching canon | AI-assisted (scraping + LLM extraction) | Syllabus readings from university courses worldwide |
| bibCNRS | Hand-harvested (CNRS credentials) | CNRS portal aggregating non-English news & discourse (Gale, Wanfang, NewsBank) in FR, ZH, JA, DE |
| SciSpace | AI-collected, hand-exported | SciSpace systematic review tool exports |

: Sources, automation level, and coverage scope. {#tbl-sources}

The search strategy uses a four-tier keyword taxonomy reflecting the evolving vocabulary of climate finance. Tier 1 consists of core terms in eight languages (English, French, Chinese, Japanese, German, Spanish, Portuguese, Arabic). Tiers 2--4 are English-only: Tier 2 covers institutional and diplomatic vocabulary (e.g., "clean development mechanism," "green climate fund"), while Tiers 3 and 4 broaden the search to climate-adjacent terms with concept-group co-occurrence filters (2-of-4 and 3-of-4 respectively) to maintain precision. The taxonomy was informed by keyword mining of {{< meta corpus_core >}} core papers (cited $\geq$ {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} times) and is defined in a version-controlled YAML configuration file.

The corpus is multilingual by design. While English dominates ({{< meta lang_english_pct >}}%), the strategy explicitly targeted French (*finance climat*, *finance climatique*), Chinese, Japanese, and German through ISTEX, bibCNRS, and multilingual OpenAlex queries. This deliberate multilingual scope, though it yields a modest non-English share, provides a foundation for cross-linguistic studies that monolingual corpora cannot support.

All API queries are bounded to publication years 1990--2024; the lower bound reflects the start of UNFCCC negotiations, before which "climate finance" had no institutional referent. Raw API responses are stored in an append-only pool as compressed JSONL files, preserving complete responses for future re-extraction without re-downloading. OpenAlex indexes RePEc, so economics working papers are covered without a separate harvest. The two hand-harvested sources (bibCNRS, SciSpace) together contribute 896 records to the raw pool before quality filtering (@tbl-quality). bibCNRS is a CNRS legacy portal aggregating news and regional databases (Gale, Wanfang, NewsBank) that capture non-English public discourse on climate finance — a dimension absent from academic databases. Their inclusion is validated by multi-source overlap: 62% of SciSpace records and 9% of bibCNRS records also appear in automated sources, confirming retrieval consistency.

### 2.2 Data Structure {#sec-data-structure}

The merge pipeline combines records from all sources through two deduplication passes: DOI-based (normalised, lowercased) and title+year matching for records lacking DOIs. When duplicates are found, the maximum citation count is retained and metadata follows a source-priority order. Boolean `from_*` columns (one per source) track which databases contributed each record, enabling provenance analysis. The `source_count` field records multi-source agreement: of the {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works, {{< meta corpus_multi_source >}} ({{< meta corpus_multi_source_pct >}}%) appear in multiple sources.

A six-flag filtering pipeline then evaluates every work. Three flags require no external data: (1) missing metadata (no title), (2) absent abstract combined with an irrelevant title (lacking domain-specific terms in any of the target languages), and (3) title blacklist matches (noise terms such as "blockchain" or "deep learning" without climate-finance context). Three flags depend on enrichment outputs: (4) citation isolation (pre-2020 works neither cited by nor citing any other corpus work), (5) semantic outlier detection (embedding distance from corpus centroid exceeding mean + 2 standard deviations), and (6) cross-encoder relevance scoring against the query "climate policy and financial mechanisms."

A separate annotation step detects near-duplicate content: coordinated multi-journal publications where the same text appears under different DOIs (e.g., the COP27 editorial published simultaneously in 60+ medical journals). Detection uses a two-pass approach (abstract prefix clustering followed by title clustering with abstract-similarity validation) and assigns a shared `near_duplicate_group` identifier. These records are kept, not removed: they are real publications, but flagging them supports transparency and enables users to control for content duplication in their analyses.

The pipeline removes flagged works unless they meet one of four protection criteria: citation count $\geq$ 50, presence in 2+ sources, within-corpus citations, or appearance in teaching syllabi. The teaching canon was built by scraping university course syllabi worldwide and extracting bibliographic references through a combination of DOI regex matching and LLM-assisted extraction, followed by fuzzy deduplication and Crossref enrichment (details in the companion technical report). Every inclusion/exclusion decision is recorded in the audit trail.

### 2.3 Quality, Completeness, and Potential Biases {#sec-quality}

@tbl-quality summarises corpus composition and metadata completeness per source. The "Refined" column counts works with a `from_*` provenance flag after deduplication and quality filtering; a single work may appear in multiple rows if discovered by several sources. %non-EN is the share of non-English works, %DOI the share with a valid DOI, %Abstract the share with an abstract longer than 50 characters, and %Refs the share with at least one reference in the citation graph.

{{< include tables/tab_corpus_sources.md >}}

The merge pipeline reduces {{< meta corpus_raw >}} raw records to {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works ({{< meta corpus_removal_pct >}}% removal rate). Of the {{< meta filter_flagged >}} works flagged (counts are non-exclusive: a single work may trigger multiple flags), citation isolation ({{< meta filter_citation_isolated >}}) and cross-encoder relevance scoring ({{< meta filter_llm_irrelevant >}}) are the most common, followed by absent abstracts with irrelevant titles ({{< meta filter_no_abstract >}}), missing metadata ({{< meta filter_missing_metadata >}}), and title blacklist matches ({{< meta filter_title_blacklist >}}). Of these, {{< meta filter_protected >}} are retained by protection criteria, yielding {{< meta filter_net_removals >}} net removals; an additional 21 content-duplicates discovered during enrichment are also removed.

As @tbl-quality shows, OpenAlex contributes {{< meta openalex_pct >}}% of refined works; the five smaller sources add non-English, institutional, and pedagogical coverage that OpenAlex alone does not provide. Citation graph verification on a sample of 300 links confirmed 99.0% against Crossref metadata (95% CI [97.1%, 99.7%]); the 1% unconfirmed are OpenAlex-sourced references absent from Crossref, not errors. A data-integrity check verified that no Crossref references were lost during harvesting (0 misses on 300 source DOIs); Crossref is a primary source for the corpus's citation graph, so this confirms the pipeline introduces no silent data loss. Restricting to links where both citing and cited works belong to the refined corpus yields {{< meta cite_refined_rows >}} corpus-internal citation pairs; {{< meta cite_refined_coverage_pct >}}% of corpus DOIs participate in at least one such pair, rising to {{< meta cite_coverage_core_pct >}}% for core papers (cited $\geq$ 50 times).

Relevance filtering (flag 6) uses a cross-encoder reranker (BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3, 568M parameters) for deterministic, reproducible scores. The query "climate policy and financial mechanisms" was selected from 20 candidates (AUC = 0.766 on weak labels); human validation on a blinded sample of 100 works yielded AUC = 0.818 and 81% accuracy. The chosen query privileges instrumental framing; works centred on climate justice without financial mechanisms vocabulary may score lower. These methods serve as independent checks on the flagging pipeline, not as ground truth, since what counts as "climate finance" is itself a contested category. The Zenodo deposit includes all calibration and validation tables.

{{< include tables/tab_languages.md >}}

Near-duplicate detection identified coordinated multi-journal publications --- notably the COP27 editorial [@atwoli_etal2022], published with identical or near-identical abstracts across 60+ journals under different DOIs. These are not data errors but a deliberate publication strategy; the `near_duplicate_group` column enables users to deduplicate by content if their analysis requires it.

Users should be aware of several potential biases. This corpus is strongest on the academic pool (Crossref, OpenAlex) and weakest on policy discourse and media. International organisations and think tanks are partially captured through the grey-literature source; news and public discourse are represented through bibCNRS. Non-English coverage remains limited (@tbl-languages): English accounts for {{< meta lang_english_pct >}}% of refined works despite targeting eight languages, reflecting structural biases in indexing systems. The enriched corpus contains works in 48 detected languages. Citation counts reflect OpenAlex data as of the collection date (2026-03-26) and favour older, English-language works. The teaching canon reflects a harvesting bias toward institutions with publicly accessible syllabi.

## 3. Data {#sec-data}

The Zenodo deposit (DOI: [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130), temporal coverage 1990--2024) is a reproducibility archive in two parts: the pipeline source code (scripts, configuration, and DVC definitions) and the v1.1 corpus data files described below. All CSV files use UTF-8 encoding with standard comma-separated format; identifiers are opaque string keys.

**climate_finance_corpus.csv** ({{< meta corpus_raw >}} rows). The complete, unfiltered corpus. Each row is one deduplicated work with columns for: identifier (string), DOI, title, first author, year (integer), citation count (integer), publication venue, work type, language code (ISO 639-1), source provenance flags (one boolean per source: `from_openalex`, `from_istex`, `from_bibcnrs`, `from_scispace`, `from_grey`, `from_teaching`), and quality-filtering metadata (`is_flagged`, `flag_reason`, `is_protected`). The quality flags are the audit trail: users reproduce our refined subset ({{< meta corpus_total >}} works) with `df[~df['is_flagged'] | df['is_protected']]`. A `near_duplicate_group` column (integer, null for ungrouped works) identifies coordinated multi-journal publications sharing near-identical content. A version-tracking flag (`in_v1`) marks works present in the v1.0 submission corpus. An enrichment step reconstructs missing abstracts from OpenAlex inverted indices and ISTEX fulltext (TEI XML), replacing boilerplate stubs where possible. Abstracts are not included in the deposited file due to publisher redistribution restrictions; they can be retrieved programmatically via the DOI or OpenAlex identifier. @fig-bars shows the temporal distribution.

**embeddings.npz.** Compressed NumPy archive (keys: `vectors` as float32 array, `keys` as string array of identifiers) containing L2-normalised (unit-length) {{< meta emb_dimensions >}}-dimensional vectors for {{< meta corpus_with_embeddings >}} of the {{< meta corpus_raw >}} works, computed by the multilingual sentence-transformer *BAAI/bge-m3*. Works without a title are excluded; works with missing or boilerplate abstracts (paywall stubs, repository metadata) are embedded from title and keywords only. Row *i* of `vectors` corresponds to `keys[i]`. Flagged works are included to support alternative filtering strategies. Each vector encodes the concatenation of title, abstract (when available and non-boilerplate), and keywords within the model's 8192-token context window (no truncation for typical abstracts). The model maps texts in English, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German into a shared semantic space, which is designed to group works by topic rather than by language. These vectors serve as direct input for BERTopic, top2vec, or other embedding-based topic models, and support cosine-similarity search.

**citations.csv.** Citation network ({{< meta cite_total_rows >}} rows). Columns: `source_doi`, `ref_doi` (both normalised, lowercased DOIs). Each row records a (citing, cited) pair where the citing work belongs to the corpus; the cited work may or may not. For corpus-internal citations only, join on the corpus identifiers. The citation network operates on DOI pairs: works without DOIs cannot be matched as citing sources, and references without DOIs are retained but cannot be deduplicated. Since 28% of records lack DOIs, citation coverage is incomplete for early and grey-literature works. Fulltext reference parsing (e.g., via GROBID) could recover additional links but is not implemented in the current pipeline.

**Source catalogs.** Per-source catalogs (`openalex_works.csv`, `istex_works.csv`, etc.) enable provenance tracking back to individual databases before merge and deduplication. The corpus does not include a dedicated Open Access or fulltext URL column; users can recover OA links via the OpenAlex API using the `source_id` field, or construct ISTEX fulltext URLs from ISTEX identifiers in the source catalogs.

![Annual publication volume in the corpus. The search window is 1990--2024; the earliest works date from 1992.](figures/fig_bars.png){#fig-bars width=100%}

Citation coverage --- the share of corpus works that appear as a citing source in the citation network --- is unevenly distributed over time: approximately 27% for works published before 2007 versus 47% for post-2015 works. This gap reflects two compounding factors: early and grey-literature works are less likely to carry DOIs or to have their reference lists indexed by Crossref and OpenAlex, and the field itself was smaller before 2007. Users conducting temporal citation-network analyses should account for this systematic undercount of early-period connectivity.

The dataset is released under CC BY 4.0. The four automated or semi-automated sources can be re-harvested with an internet connection; the two restricted sources (bibCNRS, SciSpace) have their raw exports included in the Zenodo deposit.

## 4. Concluding Remarks {#sec-concluding}

This corpus assembles climate finance scholarship into a single, cross-lingual bibliographic object. Its {{< meta corpus_sources >}}-source design pairs the breadth of OpenAlex with coverage from French archives (ISTEX), non-English discourse (bibCNRS), institutional reports (grey literature), pedagogical use (teaching canon), and AI-assisted discovery (SciSpace) absent from single-database corpora. Pre-computed multilingual embeddings lower the barrier to semantic analysis, enabling researchers to study thematic structure, temporal evolution, and cross-lingual patterns without re-encoding the full corpus.

The provenance architecture makes it straightforward to add new sources or extend the temporal window. A future iteration would benefit from OpenAlex's bulk download rather than keyword-based API queries, reducing dependence on vocabulary choices made at harvest time. By making its boundaries explicit rather than implicit, the corpus turns the definition of "climate finance" from a hidden assumption into an object of analysis.

## Data and Code Availability {.unnumbered}

The corpus data files and pipeline source code are deposited in Zenodo under CC BY 4.0 at [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130). Key software dependencies: Python $\geq$ 3.10, pandas, sentence-transformers, and DVC. Suggested citation:

> Ha-Duong, M. (2026). A Curated Corpus of Climate Finance Literature, 1990–2024: Six Sources, Multilingual Retrieval, and Grey Literature [Data set]. Zenodo. [10.5281/zenodo.19236130](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19236130)

## References {.unnumbered}
